source.title: SPACIER: on-demand polymer design with fully automated all-atom classical molecular dynamics integrated into machine learning pipelines

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the challenge of on-demand polymer design by integrating fully automated all-atom classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with Bayesian optimization (BO). The key problem is the lack of experimental data and the limitations of interpolative machine learning predictors for polymeric materials. Existing databases like PoLyInfo have limited sample sizes, especially for properties beyond basic ones. While computer experiments (e.g., DFT) have been integrated into ML pipelines for inorganic materials, automating all-atom MD for polymers is hindered by technical barriers (parameter choices, simulation protocols) and high computational costs. The hypothesis is that combining RadonPy (automated MD property calculations) with BO can efficiently explore virtual polymer libraries to discover materials with desired properties, even in unexplored chemical spaces. The workflow includes a calibration step to correct systematic biases between MD-calculated and experimental properties. The proof-of-concept targets optical polymers with high refractive index and Abbe number, aiming to surpass the empirical Pareto boundary.

section_gists.heading: SPACIER: on-demand polymer design with fully automated all-atom classical molecular dynamics integrated into machine learning pipelines
section_gists.gist: Introduces SPACIER, an open-source software that integrates RadonPy (automated all-atom MD for polymer properties) with Bayesian optimization for on-demand polymer design. Highlights the challenge of data scarcity in polymers and the technical barriers to automating MD simulations. Positions the work as a proof-of-concept for discovering optical polymers beyond the refractive index-Abbe number tradeoff.
process_signals: Motivation: lack of experimental data and limitations of interpolative ML predictors.
Constraint: automating all-atom MD for polymers is technically challenging and computationally intensive.
Hypothesis: integrating automated MD with BO can accelerate polymer discovery.
Goal: develop an open-source system (SPACIER) and demonstrate it by synthesizing optical polymers surpassing the Pareto boundary.
methods: RadonPy for automated polymer property calculations (all-atom classical MD).
Bayesian optimization for efficient exploration of virtual polymer libraries.
Calibration to correct systematic biases between MD and experiment.
failures_or_limits: Scarcity of polymer data (e.g., only ~30 samples for thermal conductivity).
Technical barriers in automating MD: many parameters, force field choices, simulation protocols.
High computational cost of all-atom MD.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Placeholder for results section; no detailed content provided.

section_gists.heading: Methods outline
section_gists.gist: Describes the SPACIER workflow: pool-based BO with GP surrogate using force-field kernel mean descriptor. RadonPy calculates Cp, refractive index, Abbe number. Linear calibration corrects systematic biases (e.g., Cp overestimation due to missing quantum effects). Candidate sets: 1077 synthetic polymers and 101,487 virtual polymers from SMiPoly. Acquisition functions: PI and EHVI for multi-objective optimization.
process_signals: Design choice: pool-based BO with GP surrogate and RBF kernel.
Calibration step: linear models to correct MD-calculated properties (Cp overestimated, refractive index underestimated).
Virtual library generation: SMiPoly with 22 reaction rules, filtered to 101,487 candidates.
Acquisition functions: multi-objective PI and EHVI.
methods: GP regression with RBF kernel.
Force-field kernel mean descriptor (170-dimensional).
RadonPy for Cp, refractive index, Abbe number.
Linear calibration: Y_e = α Y_s + β.
SMiPoly for virtual polymer generation.
Multi-objective BO with PI and EHVI.
metrics: Mean absolute errors for calibration: Cp 167.53, refractive index 0.02.
Coefficients of determination: Cp 0.61, refractive index 0.92.
failures_or_limits: RadonPy-calculated properties do not match experimental values perfectly (Cp overestimated, refractive index underestimated).
Calibration required to correct systematic biases.

section_gists.heading: Illustrative example
section_gists.gist: Demonstrates SPACIER on a toy problem targeting Cp and refractive index. Calibration shows Cp overestimation (MAE 167.53, R² 0.61) and refractive index underestimation (MAE 0.02, R² 0.92). BO outperforms Fix-GP and Random in detecting polymers in target regions within 20-30 cycles. Ablation studies show Fix-GP performance degrades with biased initial data. BO also effective with EHVI for Pareto boundary exploration.
process_signals: Calibration: linear models fitted to experimental data.
BO advantage: detects all target polymers in 20-30 cycles vs. baselines.
Ablation: Fix-GP approaches BO with large initial dataset but fails with biased data.
BO robust to target region changes and effective with EHVI.
methods: Linear calibration for Cp and refractive index.
Multi-objective PI acquisition function.
Comparison of BO, Fix-GP, Random sampling.
Ablation studies: initial dataset size, biased sampling, EHVI.
metrics: BO cycles to detect all targets: 20-30.
failures_or_limits: Cp calibration R² only 0.61, indicating limited predictive accuracy.
Fix-GP fails with biased initial data.

section_gists.heading: Optical polymers predicted and discovered by SPACIER
section_gists.gist: Applies SPACIER to discover optical polymers with high refractive index and Abbe number. BO with EHVI over 20 cycles crosses empirical Pareto boundary. 64% of designed polymers exceed limit; many contain sulfonyl groups. Three polymers selected for synthesis: P1 (dithiocarbonate), P2 (dithiocarbonate), P3 (dithiourethane). P1 synthesized successfully, film formed. P2 precipitated, insoluble, no film; copolymerization attempted but failed film formation. P3 synthesis attempted but details incomplete in text. Experimental validation for P1 shows good agreement with calibrated RadonPy predictions (refractive index: exp 1.64 vs sim 1.63; Abbe number: exp 32.0 vs sim 32.0).
process_signals: BO successfully learns design principle (sulfonyl groups) autonomously.
Synthesis challenges: P2 insoluble, no film; copolymerization did not yield film.
P1 experimental validation matches calibrated predictions well.
Calibration: linear models for refractive index (MAE 0.02, R² 0.92) and Abbe number (MAE 2.79, R² 0.96).
methods: Multi-objective BO with EHVI.
Virtual library from SMiPoly using 1021 purchasable monomers.
Linear calibration for refractive index and Abbe number.
Synthesis: P1 via CDI method; P2 attempted; P3 attempted.
Characterization: NMR, film fabrication, ellipsometry.
metrics: Calibration MAE: refractive index 0.02, Abbe number 2.79.
Calibration R²: refractive index 0.92, Abbe number 0.96.
P1 experimental vs simulated: refractive index 1.64 vs 1.63, Abbe number 32.0 vs 32.0.
failures_or_limits: P2 synthesis: polymer precipitated, insoluble, no film formed; copolymerization did not yield film.
P3 synthesis details incomplete; experimental values not reported (only simulated).
Only a few polymers significantly exceed empirical boundary due to limited structural diversity (one-step synthesis from commercial monomers).

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Reflects on SPACIER's proof-of-concept. RadonPy predictions were sufficiently predictive for the three properties, with good agreement for synthesized polymers. Limitations: unclear quantitative predictiveness for other properties/systems; linear calibration may be improved by transfer learning (e.g., affine transfer models). Synthesis bottleneck: P2 insolubility due to structural similarity to PES. Limited structural diversity because only one-step synthesis from commercial monomers was considered. Future work: expand monomer set, use sequential algorithms (genetic algorithms, deep generative models), integrate solubility predictors, and consider reactivity/crystallinity.
process_signals: Acknowledgment that RadonPy's predictiveness for other properties is uncertain.
Linear calibration is a simple approach; more advanced methods (transfer learning) could improve accuracy.
Synthesis failure (P2) attributed to insolubility, a known issue for PES-like polymers.
Limited structural diversity due to one-step synthesis constraint.
Future directions: expand virtual libraries, use sequential design algorithms, integrate solubility/reactivity predictors.
failures_or_limits: Unclear quantitative predictiveness of RadonPy for other properties/polymer systems.
Linear calibration may not capture all discrepancies; advanced calibrators suggested.
P2 synthesis failed due to insolubility; highlights need for solubility prediction.
Limited structural diversity from one-step synthesis; multi-step synthesis could yield better candidates.
Pool-based BO may be less effective than sequential algorithms for intensive exploration.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Placeholder for methods section; no detailed content provided.

section_gists.heading: Candidate polymers
section_gists.gist: Describes generation of candidate polymer sets. Illustrative example: 1077 polymers from RadonPy's GitHub. Optical polymers: 101,487 virtual polymers from SMiPoly using readily available monomers, filtered by removing cation-anion pairs, B/Si atoms, polymers without two asterisks in SMILES, redundant repeating units, large repeating units (>55 atoms), and overlap with the 1077 set.
process_signals: Design choice: use SMiPoly for virtual library generation with rule-based polymerization.
Filtering steps to ensure synthetic accessibility and manage computational cost (e.g., atom count limit).
Exclusion of certain elements (B, Si) and ion pairs to focus on plausible polymers.
methods: SMiPoly with 22 reaction rules.
Filtering: remove cation-anion pairs, B, Si; require two asterisks in SMILES; remove duplicates; atom count ≤55; exclude 1077 known polymers.
failures_or_limits: Restriction to one-step synthesis from commercial monomers limits structural diversity.
Atom count limit (≤55) may exclude some interesting polymers.

section_gists.heading: Bayesian optimization
section_gists.gist: Summarizes BO procedure: derivative-free optimization of black-box function f. Starts with initial dataset, uses acquisition function to select next query from candidate pool, observes output via computer experiment, updates surrogate model. Algorithm 1 referenced but not detailed.
process_signals: Standard BO loop: initial data, acquisition, query, update.
Pool-based setting: candidates are pre-defined.
methods: Bayesian optimization with GP surrogate.
Acquisition function guides selection.

section_gists.heading: Surrogate models
section_gists.gist: Uses GP regression with RBF kernel from scikit-learn. Kernel includes scaling parameters and noise term. Hyperparameters determined by maximum likelihood estimation at each BO step.
process_signals: Choice of GP with RBF kernel as surrogate.
Hyperparameters optimized via MLE at each step.
methods: GP regression with RBF kernel: k(X,X') = θ1² exp(-||X-X'||²/(2θ2²)) + θ3·I(X=X').
Hyperparameter estimation: maximum likelihood.

section_gists.heading: Calibration
section_gists.gist: Linear calibration: Y_e = α Y_s + β. Cp calibrated with 72 experimental values from PoLyInfo. Refractive index and Abbe number calibrated with 26 polymers from literature.
process_signals: Simple linear calibration to correct systematic biases.
Calibration datasets: 72 for Cp, 26 for optical properties.
methods: Linear regression: Y_e = α Y_s + β.
Least squares fitting.
failures_or_limits: Limited calibration data (26 for optical properties) may affect generalizability.
Linear model may not capture complex discrepancies.

section_gists.heading: Polymer physical property calculations
section_gists.gist: Details RadonPy's automated MD workflow: conformation search, DFT atomic charges, force field assignment (GAFF2), amorphous cell generation, equilibration (Larsen's 21-step protocol, NpT 5-20 ns), property calculation. Refractive index from Lorentz-Lorenz equation using DFT polarizability. Abbe number from wavelength-dependent refractive indices using sum-over-states TD-DFT. Computational cost tradeoff: truncate excited states to 0.3% (a=0.003) due to prohibitive cost of full TD-DFT; Abbe numbers underestimated but correlation with experiment remains 0.98.
process_signals: Automated MD workflow with standardized presets.
Refractive index calculation relies on DFT polarizability and MD density.
Abbe number calculation requires TD-DFT, which is computationally expensive.
Tradeoff: truncating excited states reduces cost but underestimates Abbe number; correlation with experiment remains high (0.98).
Choice of a=0.003 as compromise between accuracy and cost.
methods: RadonPy v0.2.5: automated MD with GAFF2, LAMMPS, Psi4.
Equilibration: Larsen's 21-step protocol, NpT 5-20 ns at 300 K, 1 atm.
Refractive index: Lorentz-Lorenz equation with DFT polarizability (ωB97M-D3BJ/6-311+G(2d,p)).
Abbe number: sum-over-states TD-DFT with CAM-B3LYP/6-311+G(2d,p), truncated excited states (a=0.003).
metrics: Correlation coefficient for Abbe number with a=0.003: 0.98.
failures_or_limits: Full TD-DFT computationally prohibitive for large molecules.
Truncating excited states leads to underestimated Abbe numbers.
Cp overestimation due to missing quantum effects in classical MD.
Refractive index underestimation likely due to density underestimation.

section_gists.heading: Experimental validation
section_gists.gist: Describes experimental methods for synthesized polymers: NMR, FT-IR, SEC, TGA, DSC, spectroscopic ellipsometry. Lists reagents used.
process_signals: Standard polymer characterization techniques.
Reagents sourced commercially.
methods: NMR (400 MHz), FT-IR, SEC (THF, 40°C, polystyrene standards), TGA (N2, 10°C/min), DSC (10°C/min), ellipsometry.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The paper hypothesizes that integrating automated all-atom MD (RadonPy) with Bayesian optimization can overcome data scarcity and enable on-demand polymer design.
It assumes that systematic biases in MD-calculated properties can be corrected via linear calibration, making the surrogate model sufficiently accurate for guiding experiments.
The workflow is expected to autonomously learn design principles (e.g., sulfonyl groups for high refractive index) and discover polymers beyond empirical limits.
The proof-of-concept aims to demonstrate that SPACIER can predict and synthesize novel optical polymers with validated properties.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How generalizable is the linear calibration? Would transfer learning or affine models significantly improve predictions for other properties?
To what extent are RadonPy-calculated properties quantitatively predictive for other polymer systems and properties beyond Cp, refractive index, and Abbe number?
Can the synthesis failures (e.g., P2 insolubility) be mitigated by integrating solubility predictors or by expanding the virtual library to include multi-step synthesis?
Would sequential design algorithms (e.g., genetic algorithms, deep generative models) outperform pool-based BO in exploring chemical space?
How does the truncation of excited states in TD-DFT affect the reliability of Abbe number predictions for diverse polymers?
What is the scalability of SPACIER to larger virtual libraries and more complex polymer architectures (e.g., copolymers)?

events.summary: Lack of experimental data and limitations of interpolative ML predictors hinder polymer design.
Technical barriers in automating all-atom MD simulations for polymers hinder BO-aided computational design.
SPACIER integrates RadonPy (automated all-atom MD) with Bayesian optimization for polymer design.
Pool-based BO with GP surrogate and force-field kernel mean descriptor selects candidates; RadonPy calculates properties.
Linear calibration corrects systematic biases between RadonPy-calculated and experimental properties.
Virtual polymer library generated using SMiPoly with 22 reaction rules, filtered to 101,487 candidates.
Multi-objective BO uses PI or EHVI acquisition functions to search for polymers with desired properties or Pareto boundary.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk introduces the problem, design of SPACIER, and key methodological choices. It contains explicit process events: problem statements, design decisions, and a limitation.

events.summary: RadonPy-calculated Cp overestimates experimental values due to missing quantum effects; refractive index underestimates due to density underestimation.
Linear calibration models fitted to correct systematic biases in Cp and refractive index.
BO detected all polymers in three target regions within 20-30 cycles, outperforming Fix-GP and Random.
Ablation study: Fix-GP performance approaches BO with large initial dataset but degrades with biased initial data.
Multi-objective BO with EHVI crossed empirical Pareto frontier for refractive index and Abbe number; 64% of designed polymers exceeded limit.
SPACIER autonomously learned that sulfonyl groups are a promising design principle for high refractive index polymers.
P2 synthesis failed: polymer precipitated, insoluble, no film formed; copolymerization did not yield film.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers illustrative example and optical polymer discovery, including calibration, BO performance, synthesis attempts, and validation.

events.summary: Synthesis of P2 polymer failed due to precipitation and insolubility, preventing film formation.
Instead of measuring P3, the authors used reported values of a structural analog mpPh-PTU.
Experimental and calculated refractive indices and Abbe numbers for P1 and mpPh-PTU are in good agreement.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental synthesis outcomes and validation of calculated properties against experiments.

events.summary: P1 was successfully synthesized and formed a film; P2 precipitated and was insoluble, failing to form a film.
Only a few optical polymers significantly exceeded the empirical boundary due to limited structural diversity from restricting to one-step synthesis from commercial monomers.
It remains unclear to what extent RadonPy-calculated properties are quantitatively predictive for other properties and polymer systems.
A machine learning solubility predictor could be used to exclude poorly soluble polymers in advance, improving the virtual library quality.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental synthesis results, validation data, and discussion of limitations and future revisions.

events.summary: Wavelength-dependent polarizability calculated using sum-over-states approach because coupled-perturbed Hartree-Fock not implemented for DFT in Psi4.
High computational cost of full TD-DFT addressed by truncating excited states; tradeoff investigated.
Preliminary calculations compared experimental and calculated Abbe numbers for 26 polymers at different truncation levels.
At a=0.3, Abbe numbers agreed closely with experiment, but computational cost prohibitive for larger molecules.
With fewer excited states (a=0.003), Abbe numbers underestimated but correlation with experiment remained 0.98.
Proceeded with TD-DFT calculations considering 0.3% of all excited states (a=0.003) as compromise.
Synthesis of P2: polymer obtained but insoluble in common organic solvents.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains computational methodology details (TD-DFT truncation tradeoff) and experimental synthesis outcome (P2 insolubility), both relevant to process events.
This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, competing interests, and additional information. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: A major challenge in data-driven materials research is the lack of data resources.
the scarcity of data on polymeric materials is remarkable.
technical barriers in automating and accelerating all-atom molecular simulations have hindered the BO-aided computational design of polymeric materials.

We developed an autonomous polymer design tool, materials SPACe frontIER (SPACIER), which integrates fully automated polymer physical property calculations based on all-atom classical MD simulations into a BO-accelerated material design pipeline.
With a given library of virtual polymers generated as described later, a pool-based BO was applied to identify promising candidates with the desired properties.
A linear calibrator was derived from the experimental and calculated data to collect the systematic bias.
The calibrator was constructed before performing BO.
SMiPoly is a virtual polymer generator that implements 22 polymerization reaction rules
SPACIER implements the probability of improvement (PI) and expected improvement as acquisition functions for ordinary single-objective optimization.
the calculated values for $C_{p}$ overestimate the experimental values because of the absence of quantum effects in classical MD simulations
The refractive index calculated using RadonPy slightly underestimates the experimental values, likely owing to an underestimation of the density
The linear models were fitted to the experimental values to correct for these systematic biases
The mean absolute errors were 167.53 and 0.02, and the coefficients of determination were 0.61 and 0.92 for the $C_{\mathrm{p}}$ and refractive index, respectively.
BO detected all polymers in the three target regions within 20–30 cycles (Fig. 3b), demonstrating a clear advantage over Fix-GP and Random.
When increasing the initial dataset size to 100, the detection performance of Fix-GP approached that of BO
when the initial dataset was sampled from a biased region with low $C_p$ and a refractive index, the performance of Fix-GP was significantly lower
During 20 cycles of the multi-objective BO, the designed polymers gradually approached and eventually crossed the empirically known Pareto frontier
The percentage of all polymers crossing the empirical limit line was 64 %.
Approximately one-quarter of these polymers contained sulfur atoms with several sulfonyl groups ( $-SO_{2}-$ ) as substructures.
SPACIER has successfully learned this design principle autonomously.
During P2 synthesis, a solid was precipitated during the polymerization reaction. P2 was insoluble in commonly used solvents
the copolymer did not successfully form a film.
the synthesis of the desired polymer could be difficult because of the low nucleophilicity of the aromatic dithiol
instead of measuring the physical properties of P3, we referred to the reported physical property values of mpPh-PTU
The values of the refractive indices and Abbe numbers for P1 and mpPh-PTU are in good agreement.
the experimental values were 1.64 for P1 and 1.81 for mpPh-PTU, while the calculated values were 1.63 and 1.84
the experimental values were 32.0 for P1 and 11.0 for mpPh-PTU, while the calculated values were
one polymer P2, although likely synthesized, failed to form a film owing to its insolubility in organic solvents.

Only a few optical polymers have been found to significantly exceed the empirical boundary. This limitation was primarily due to the lack of structural diversity in the candidate polymers because we restricted our investigation to polymers that could, in principle, be synthesized in one step from commercially available monomers.
it remains unclear to what extent the RadonPy-calculated properties are quantitatively predictive for other properties and polymer systems.
Excluding poorly soluble polymers in advance would allow the construction of a high-quality virtual library. The use of a machine learning solubility predictor can help address this issue.
the wavelength-dependent polarizability $\alpha_{ij}(\omega)$ at frequency $\omega$ was calculated using the sum-over-states approach

the high computational cost makes it impractical to calculate all one-electron excited states using TD-DFT. The tradeoff between computational accuracy and cost was achieved by truncating the number of calculated excited states
Preliminary calculations investigated the effect of the number of calculated excited states on the calculated Abbe number accuracy.

When considering up to 30% of all excited states (a = 0.3), the results agreed closely with the experimental observations, demonstrating the validity of this calculation condition. However, under a = 0.3, the computational cost became prohibitive as the molecular size increased
Although the Abbe numbers were underestimated, the correlation coefficient with the experimental values remained at 0.98 for a = 0.003
we proceeded with the TD-DFT calculations considering 0.3% of all the excited states (a = 0.003).
P2 was obtained as a white solid (0.84 g). This polymer was insoluble in common organic solvents.

In each BO step, the top 10 polymers of the acquisition function were selected from the candidate polymers, which were polymerized using SMiPoly from 1021 purchasable compounds.

Furthermore, linking with sophisticated external molecule generators such as SMiPoly $^{32}$ , a virtual library generator based on polymerization reaction rules, makes it possible to design highly synthesizable polymers while guiding their synthetic routes.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: On-demand polymer design is hindered by data scarcity and the difficulty of automating all-atom MD simulations for property prediction.

canonical_events.summary: Lack of experimental data and limitations of interpolative ML predictors hinder polymer design.
Technical barriers in automating all-atom MD simulations for polymers hinder BO-aided computational design.
SPACIER integrates RadonPy (automated all-atom MD) with Bayesian optimization for polymer design.
Pool-based BO with GP surrogate and force-field kernel mean descriptor selects candidates; RadonPy calculates properties.
Linear calibration corrects systematic biases between RadonPy-calculated and experimental properties.
Virtual polymer library generated using SMiPoly with 22 reaction rules, filtered to 101,487 candidates.
Multi-objective BO uses PI or EHVI acquisition functions to search for polymers with desired properties or Pareto boundary.
RadonPy-calculated Cp overestimates experimental values due to missing quantum effects; refractive index underestimates due to density underestimation.
Linear calibration models fitted to correct systematic biases in Cp and refractive index.
BO detected all polymers in three target regions within 20-30 cycles, outperforming Fix-GP and Random.
Ablation study: Fix-GP performance approaches BO with large initial dataset but degrades with biased initial data.
Multi-objective BO with EHVI crossed empirical Pareto frontier for refractive index and Abbe number; 64% of designed polymers exceeded limit.
SPACIER autonomously learned that sulfonyl groups are a promising design principle for high refractive index polymers.
P2 synthesis failed: polymer precipitated, insoluble, no film formed; copolymerization did not yield film.
Experimental and calculated refractive indices and Abbe numbers for P1 and mpPh-PTU are in good agreement.
Only a few optical polymers significantly exceeded the empirical boundary due to limited structural diversity from restricting to one-step synthesis from commercial monomers.
It remains unclear to what extent RadonPy-calculated properties are quantitatively predictive for other properties and polymer systems.
A machine learning solubility predictor could be used to exclude poorly soluble polymers in advance, improving the virtual library quality.

edges.reason: Both are problem statements; data scarcity is followed by technical barriers in MD automation.
The technical barriers motivate the design of SPACIER to automate MD and integrate with BO.
SPACIER's design is refined by specifying pool-based BO with GP surrogate and descriptor.
The BO pipeline is refined by adding a linear calibration step to correct MD biases.
Calibration is set up before generating the virtual library for BO.
The virtual library is used with multi-objective BO acquisition functions.
Before applying BO, the systematic biases in RadonPy properties are analyzed.
The analysis of biases leads to fitting linear calibration models.
Calibrated models are used in BO, which outperforms baselines in detecting target polymers.
Ablation study further analyzes BO performance under different conditions.
After illustrative example, multi-objective BO is applied to optical polymers, crossing Pareto frontier.
Crossing the Pareto frontier leads to autonomous learning of sulfonyl group design principle.
Despite learning design principles, synthesis of P2 fails due to insolubility.
After synthesis failures, experimental validation of P1 shows good agreement with predictions.
Validation leads to recognizing limitation of limited structural diversity.
Another limitation is the unclear predictiveness of RadonPy for other properties.
To address limitations, a revision suggests using a solubility predictor.

must_cover_points: SPACIER integration of RadonPy and BO
Pool-based BO with GP and calibration
BO outperforms baselines in illustrative example
BO crosses Pareto frontier for optical polymers
Autonomous learning of sulfonyl group design principle
